content.text:
## Recommendation

Use **"team members"** as the single primary term throughout the handbook, with one defined-term clause near the front linking it to "employee" as used in the legal documents. This aligns the handbook with the firm's 7-year outward voice (point 4), matches the clear staff preference (67%, point 6), sits comfortably within common industry practice (22% of peer firms, point 3), and carries the modest morale/hierarchy benefit documented in the research (point 1) — all without creating new mismatches or legal exposure.

## Reasoning chain

"Team members" is the only option that reduces rather than adds internal/external inconsistency. The firm has used it externally for seven years, and new hires encounter it before any other term during onboarding (point 4). Switching the handbook to "colleagues" would create a third mismatch; keeping "employees" leaves the existing voice/document gap unresolved. "Team members" closes the gap.

The staff signal points the same way: 67% chose "team members" versus 21% for "colleagues" and 12% for "employees" (point 6). Even discounting for the non-anonymous survey design — which would tend to inflate support for whatever staff think leadership favors and suppress dissent — "team members" is the safe, low-risk popular choice. The write-in comments reinforce that it reflects how the company already talks ("everyone says it now," "fits how the company already talks").

The research (point 1) shows "team members" produces a small but consistent reduction in felt hierarchy with no change to actual authority — so it delivers whatever symbolic value is realistically available. The chair's case for "colleagues" rests on the same mechanism but for a word only 4% of peer firms use (point 3) and only 21% of staff prefer.

The chair's strongest argument is fit with pay philosophy (point 5). But that argument is weaker than it appears: published pay ranges with performance-based placement explicitly describe a structured, leveled progression — which is not a flat peer relationship. "Colleagues" overstates the equality the comp structure actually embodies, and the research confirms the word would not change any real reporting line or authority. "Team members" is consistent enough with a peer-toned culture without implying a flatness the pay system doesn't deliver.

## Concerns / risks

This choice does not satisfy the comp-committee chair, who is a founding partner and may continue to push for "colleagues." Adopting "team members" is partly overruling a partner's stated preference, which is a governance/relationship cost the word choice itself can't resolve — that needs a direct conversation, not a handbook edit.

The 67% figure is from a named, non-anonymous survey, so it is softer evidence than it looks; real preference is uncertain. I am still recommending "team members" because it is also the lowest-risk and most consistent option, so the recommendation doesn't hinge on that number being precise.

"Team members" carries a faint informality that outside lawyers reading the handbook may find less crisp than "employees." The defined-term clause mitigates this but doesn't eliminate the stylistic difference.

Finally, the benefit is genuinely small (1–3 points per the research). No option here meaningfully changes culture or structure on its own; anyone expecting the word to reinforce flatness or pay philosophy in a real way will be disappointed regardless of choice.

On the legal-document issue (point 2): include a single clause early in the handbook — e.g., "In this handbook, 'team members' refers to the individuals TerraClarity employs; their legal employment status is 'employee' as defined in their offer letters and applicable law, and nothing in this handbook alters the at-will nature of that employment." This neutralizes the mismatch-as-evidence risk from the *Toussaint* line of cases while letting the handbook use the preferred term throughout. This clause should be added whichever word is chosen.

## Confidence

Medium-high: the evidence converges cleanly on "team members," but the small effect sizes and the non-anonymous survey mean the upside is modest and the staff-preference signal is softer than its headline number suggests.
